Question: Is all pre-incorporation IP properly assignable to the company?
Description: Any code, design, copy, or other creative work produced before the entity exists is owned by whoever created it — not the company. This includes founder work and especially any contractor or freelancer contributions. Without proper assignment at incorporation, the company has a cloud on title that will surface in fundraising or acquisition diligence.
Mitigation: Identify every contributor (including the founder pre-formation), execute IP assignment agreements at formation. For contractors, get signed PIIAs or rebuild that work. Counsel-drafted assignment as part of formation package.

Question: Is there any pre-existing co-founder or contributor equity expectation that has not been documented?
Description: Verbal promises of equity to early collaborators friends or advisors create future claims. Even unwritten understandings can become litigation in success scenarios. Acquirers and investors will ask about this directly.
Mitigation: Founder must catalog every conversation where equity was discussed or implied. Resolve each via written agreement or written confirmation that no equity is owed. Document before any external financing.

Question: Is the founder a single point of failure for critical credentials?
Description: If the founder loses access, is incapacitated, or has a falling-out with the company, who can recover DNS, domains, banking, AWS, GitHub, email admin? Most early startups have zero contingency planning here.
Mitigation: Establish credential vault with company ownership not founder personal. Designate a backup access holder (counsel or trusted advisor) for critical systems. Document recovery procedures.

Question: Will the planned product name survive trademark scrutiny?
Description: Common-law trademark conflicts may exist that USPTO database search will not surface. Domain availability does not equal trademark availability. Discovering a conflict after public launch forces rebrand — expensive and brand-damaging.
Mitigation: Comprehensive clearance search by TM counsel before any public-facing use of the name. Includes USPTO common-law state registrations and unregistered marks in commerce.

Question: Is there GPL or AGPL contamination in the current codebase?
Description: Use of GPL/AGPL-licensed open source software in proprietary code can force open-sourcing of the entire product. Acquirers run automated license scans during diligence. Discovery late creates remediation cost or kills deal.
Mitigation: Run automated license scan (FOSSA GitHub dependency graph or similar) on full codebase. Document every OSS dependency and its license. Replace any GPL/AGPL dependencies with permissively-licensed alternatives.

Question: Does the product create AI output liability exposure?
Description: If the product generates content using AI models, the company may be liable for hallucinated information, defamation, copyright infringement in generated output, or harm caused by user reliance on outputs. Standard TOS limitations may not cover.
Mitigation: Counsel review of liability framework once product output behavior is defined. Specific TOS language disclaiming reliance and limiting liability. Consider AI-specific cyber liability coverage when available.
Linked Initiatives: Draft Customer Terms of Service and Privacy Policy

Question: Does the company have a single point of failure on technical knowledge?
Description: Does anyone other than the founder/lead developer understand the production architecture, deployment process, and critical configuration? If that person is unavailable, can the company operate?
Mitigation: Document architecture in writing. Conduct one operational walkthrough with a second person (advisor counsel or trusted contractor under NDA). Maintain runbooks for critical procedures.

Question: Does Florida sales tax apply to the planned revenue model?
Description: Florida does not tax most SaaS but does tax data processing services and information services. The line between non-taxable software access and taxable data processing is unclear. Florida Department of Revenue has aggressive enforcement on misclassification.
Mitigation: CPA review of specific revenue model once defined. Document position and reasoning. Sales tax registration if any portion of revenue is taxable. Voluntary disclosure agreement available if past liability exists.
Linked Initiatives: Set Up Florida Sales Tax Registration

Question: Does the Florida Digital Bill of Rights apply to the company?
Description: FDBR effective July 2024 applies to controllers processing data of 100,000+ Florida consumers or 25,000+ where 50%+ revenue from data sales. Lower threshold than CCPA. Compliance failure creates AG enforcement risk.
Mitigation: Counsel review once user base approaches threshold. Privacy policy must reflect FDBR rights if applicable. Most early startups do not hit threshold but should monitor.
Linked Initiatives: Draft Customer Terms of Service and Privacy Policy

Question: Are personal and business activities properly separated for liability purposes?
Description: Mixing personal and business expenses through founder accounts pierces the corporate veil and creates tax problems. Common founder error in first 6 months.
Mitigation: Open business bank account immediately after EIN. Use only business cards for business expenses. Reimburse personal expenditures through documented expense reports.

Question: Is the technical architecture creating compliance debt for future enterprise sales?
Description: Choices made now in data handling, logging, access controls, and infrastructure can create expensive remediation when SOC2 or enterprise procurement requires them. Building right initially is cheaper than retrofitting.
Mitigation: Awareness only at this stage. Tag this for re-review once enterprise customer interest emerges or product architecture is locked in.
